schools.summary: The LORD appears at Mamre: 'and the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre: and he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day' — the LORD appearing to Abraham at his dwelling at Mamre (the oaks/terebinths of Mamre, 13:18; 14:13, the theophany, in which the LORD, with two angels, vv.2, 22; 19:1, came to Abraham, the gracious visit of God to his servant); Abraham 'sat in the tent door in the heat of the day' (the patriarch, a tent-dweller, Hebrews 11:9, sitting at his tent door in the midday heat, resting, or watching for travelers to show hospitality, the homely setting of the divine visit, the patriarch at rest when God drew near); the LORD's appearing to Abraham (in human form, vv.2-8, eating with him) a remarkable condescension (God communing familiarly with his friend, James 2:23, the visit that would bring the renewed promise of Isaac, v.10, and the disclosure of Sodom's doom, v.17, the gracious nearness of God to the patriarch) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD appeared unto him in the plains of Mamre: and he sat in the tent door in the heat of the day
the LORD's appearing to Abraham at Mamre
the gracious visit of God to his servant (with two angels)
the condescension of God communing familiarly with his friend (James 2:23)

schools.summary: The three men: 'and he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him: and when he saw them, he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself toward the ground' — three men appearing, in human form; the dominant Christian reading holding that one of the three was the LORD himself (the Angel of the LORD, a pre-incarnate appearance of the Son of God, who remains with Abraham, vv.13, 17, 22), and the other two angels (who go on to Sodom, 19:1), the LORD with two attendant angels; the Christian Fathers seeing in the three men (addressed as one LORD) a hint or foreshadowing of the Trinity (three Persons, one God), used in the lessons for Trinity Sunday, though Ellicott cautioning that we must be careful in pressing this; the Jewish commentators taking the three as three angels (called 'men' because clothed in human form, appearing as men, though not needing food, v.8); Abraham 'ran to meet them... and bowed himself toward the ground' (the patriarch hastening to welcome the strangers and bowing in courteous, reverent greeting, the eastern custom of hospitality, the readiness and humility of Abraham in receiving the guests, entertaining angels unawares, Hebrews 13:2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Clarke.

sides.position: The LORD himself (the Angel of the LORD, a pre-incarnate Christ) with two angels who go on to Sodom (the dominant Christian reading)
Three angels (called 'men' because in human form) — the Jewish view
A hint or foreshadowing of the Trinity (three men, one LORD) — the patristic reading, pressed with caution

themes: And he lift up his eyes and looked, and, lo, three men stood by him: and when he saw them, he ran to meet them from the tent door, and bowed himself toward the ground
the three men appearing to Abraham (the crux of their identity)
the LORD with two angels vs three angels vs a hint of the Trinity
the patriarch's eager, reverent hospitality (Hebrews 13:2)

schools.summary: Abraham entreats the guests to stay: 'and said, My Lord, if now I have found favour in thy sight, pass not away, I pray thee, from thy servant' — Abraham addressing the chief of the three (the LORD, v.2) as 'My Lord,' entreating that they not pass by but stay (the humble, earnest request of hospitality, the patriarch pressing the guests to accept his welcome); 'My Lord' (the address to the principal guest, who was the LORD himself, whether Abraham at first perceived the divine presence, or addressed him as an honored guest and came to perceive it, the reverent address to the LORD among the three); 'pass not away, I pray thee, from thy servant' (Abraham entreating the guests not to pass on but to stay and be refreshed, the eager, pressing hospitality of the patriarch, the desire to honor and serve the strangers, the courtesy and warmth of Abraham's welcome) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And said, My Lord, if now I have found favour in thy sight, pass not away, I pray thee, from thy servant
Abraham's entreaty that the guests stay
the reverent address to the LORD among the three
the eager, pressing hospitality of the patriarch

schools.summary: Abraham offers water and rest: 'let a little water, I pray you, be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree' — Abraham offering the guests water to wash their feet (the eastern custom of refreshing travelers, whose feet were dusty and weary, sandalled in the heat) and rest under the tree (the shade of the oaks of Mamre, the hospitable provision for the wayfarers, the courtesy of the patriarch); the washing of the feet a customary refreshment for travelers in the hot, dusty land (the host's care for the comfort of his guests, the homely, gracious hospitality of Abraham); 'rest yourselves under the tree' (the shade of the tree offered for the guests' rest in the heat of the day, v.1, the patriarch providing shade, water, and, vv.5-8, food, the full hospitality of Abraham to the strangers, the eager, generous welcome) — Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: Let a little water, I pray you, be fetched, and wash your feet, and rest yourselves under the tree
Abraham offering water and rest to the guests
the eastern custom of refreshing weary travelers
the homely, generous hospitality of Abraham

schools.summary: Abraham offers food, the guests accept: 'and I will fetch a morsel of bread, and comfort ye your hearts; after that ye shall pass on: for therefore are ye come to your servant. And they said, So do, as thou hast said' — Abraham offering the guests food (a 'morsel of bread,' modestly understated, but in fact a generous meal, vv.6-8) to refresh and strengthen them (the hospitable provision of food, the patriarch's eagerness to serve, calling the abundant meal a 'morsel'); 'after that ye shall pass on; for therefore are ye come to your servant' (Abraham urging that, after refreshment, they might continue their journey, he taking their coming as a providential opportunity to serve them, the patriarch valuing the chance to show hospitality); 'they said, So do, as thou hast said' (the guests accepting Abraham's hospitality, the LORD and the angels consenting to stay and eat, vv.6-8, the gracious acceptance of the patriarch's welcome, the condescension of the divine guests to be served by Abraham) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And I will fetch a morsel of bread, and comfort ye your hearts... And they said, So do, as thou hast said
Abraham offering food and the guests' acceptance
the generous meal modestly called a 'morsel'
the divine guests condescending to be served by Abraham

schools.summary: Abraham hastens to prepare bread: 'and Abraham hastened into the tent unto Sarah, and said, Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes upon the hearth' — Abraham hurrying to have a meal prepared (bidding Sarah quickly make cakes, bread, of three measures of fine meal, the haste and eagerness of the patriarch's hospitality, the bustle of preparation for the honored guests); the repeated marks of haste — 'hastened,' 'quickly' (the eager, prompt hospitality of Abraham, the patriarch and his household hurrying to serve the guests, the readiness and warmth of the welcome, an example of cheerful, ungrudging hospitality); 'three measures of fine meal' baked into cakes (a generous quantity of the best flour, the abundant, ready provision, the freshly-baked bread for the guests, the liberality of the patriarch, the 'morsel,' v.5, proving a generous meal) — Gill, Clarke, Henry.

themes: And Abraham hastened into the tent unto Sarah, and said, Make ready quickly three measures of fine meal, knead it, and make cakes upon the hearth
Abraham hastening to prepare bread for the guests
the eager, prompt, ungrudging hospitality of the patriarch
the generous provision of the best flour

schools.summary: Abraham fetches a calf: 'and Abraham ran unto the herd, and fetcht a calf tender and good, and gave it unto a young man; and he hasted to dress it' — Abraham hurrying to the herd, choosing a tender, good calf, and having a servant prepare it quickly (the patriarch's eager personal exertion, running himself, and the haste of the household, the generous provision of meat for the guests); Abraham choosing 'a calf tender and good' (the best of the herd, a young, choice animal, the liberality of the patriarch, sparing nothing for his guests, the generous, choice provision); 'he hasted to dress it' (the servant hurrying to prepare, dress, cook, the calf, the repeated marks of haste, vv.6-7, showing the eager, prompt hospitality of Abraham's whole household, the patriarch and his servants hurrying together to serve the guests, the warmth and readiness of the welcome) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Abraham ran unto the herd, and fetcht a calf tender and good, and gave it unto a young man; and he hasted to dress it
Abraham fetching a calf to prepare for the guests
the patriarch's eager personal exertion and the household's haste
the choice, generous provision of meat

schools.summary: The meal set before the guests: 'and he took butter, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them; and he stood by them under the tree, and they did eat' — Abraham setting the full meal before the guests: curds (butter), milk, and the dressed calf, with the cakes (v.6, the abundant, hospitable fare, the patriarch serving his guests with the best he had); Abraham 'stood by them under the tree' (the patriarch himself waiting on his guests, standing by them as they ate, the custom of the host serving and attending, the humility and honor of Abraham, the great man personally serving the strangers, the courtesy of the patriarch); 'they did eat' (the guests, the LORD and the angels, in human form, eating the food set before them, whether they truly ate, the food really consumed, by a special act, or appeared to eat, as angels do not need food, the eating being in appearance, the divine visitors condescending to partake of Abraham's hospitality, the reality of the visit) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And he took butter, and milk, and the calf which he had dressed, and set it before them; and he stood by them under the tree, and they did eat
the meal set before the guests, who ate
the patriarch personally serving the divine visitors
the guests condescending to partake (truly or in appearance)

schools.summary: The guests ask for Sarah: 'and they said unto him, Where is Sarah thy wife? And he said, Behold, in the tent' — the guests asking after Sarah by name (the calling of Sarah by name, a stranger knowing the wife's name, a token of the divine knowledge of the visitors, the question that prepared for the renewed promise of a son, v.10, the LORD's regard to Sarah, who was to bear the promised seed); the question — asking where Sarah was — not for information (the guests knew) but to draw attention to her, that she might hear the promise (v.10, the LORD's purpose to include Sarah, the matriarch's part in the promise marked); 'Behold, in the tent' (Abraham answering that Sarah was in the tent, the women's place, retired, Sarah within, near enough to hear, v.10, the patriarch's answer, the setting for Sarah to overhear the promise and laugh, vv.10-12) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And they said unto him, Where is Sarah thy wife? And he said, Behold, in the tent
the guests' asking for Sarah
the calling of Sarah by name, a token of the divine knowledge
drawing attention to Sarah for the promise of a son

schools.summary: The renewed promise of a son: 'and he said, I will certainly return unto thee according to the time of life; and, lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son. And Sarah heard it in the tent door, which was behind him' — the LORD (the chief guest) promising to return at the appointed time (the time of life, that is, when the year had run its course, the time of birth, this season next year, the renewed, definite promise of the son, 17:21, the certainty, 'certainly,' of the promise); 'lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son' (the LORD declaring that Sarah, now old, v.11, would have a son, the express promise of Isaac through Sarah, 17:19, 21, the assurance, against the human impossibility, that the matriarch would bear the promised seed, the divine word that only God could fulfill); 'Sarah heard it in the tent door, which was behind him' (Sarah, in the tent behind the speaker, overhearing the promise, the matriarch's hearing setting the stage for her laugh, v.12, the LORD's purpose that Sarah should hear the promise, the providence in her overhearing) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said, I will certainly return unto thee according to the time of life; and, lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son
the renewed promise of a son at the set time
the definite promise of Isaac through Sarah (17:19, 21), against the impossibility
Sarah overhearing the promise in the tent

schools.summary: The great age and the impossibility: 'now Abraham and Sarah were old and well stricken in age; and it ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women' — Abraham (99) and Sarah (89) very old, past the natural age of childbearing, and Sarah having passed the time of women (the cessation of the natural function, the human impossibility of the promise by nature, the barrenness now compounded by age); the great age of both — past the time of begetting and bearing — making the promise of a son humanly impossible (the natural impossibility against which the promise stood, the trial of faith, that God promised a son to those past the age); the cessation of the natural function in Sarah making conception, by nature, impossible (the promise, v.10, fulfillable only by God's almighty power, v.14, the human impossibility magnifying the divine work, the setting for Sarah's laugh, v.12, and the LORD's word, 'Is anything too hard for the LORD?', v.14) — Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: Now Abraham and Sarah were old and well stricken in age; and it ceased to be with Sarah after the manner of women
the great age of Abraham and Sarah and the natural impossibility of the promise
the promise of a son to those past the age, humanly impossible
fulfillable only by God's almighty power (v.14)

schools.summary: Calvin, Gill, and many (the laugh of incredulity/unbelief): 'therefore Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?' — Sarah laughing silently, inwardly (the laugh, in part, of incredulity and distrust — Sarah, considering her age and the impossibility, v.11, doubting the promise, the laugh of unbelief, which the LORD reproved, vv.13-15, though some allow a measure of pleasure or surprise mixed with the doubt, the predominant sense, and the LORD's reproof, marking her laugh as the laugh of incredulity, distinct from Abraham's laugh of faith, 17:17); the incredulity of Sarah not to be excused (her laugh springing from a want of faith, weighing the promise by nature rather than by God's power); Sarah's words ('After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?') expressing the natural impossibility (both she and Abraham being old, her laugh and her words weighing the promise by the course of nature, finding it incredible, the doubt of unbelief, looking to the impossibility rather than to God who promised, yet the apostle, Hebrews 11:11, noting that Sarah, by faith, received strength to conceive, her initial incredulity giving way to faith); 'my lord' (Sarah, even in her doubt, calling Abraham 'my lord,' a token of her respect and submission to her husband, commended, 1 Peter 3:6) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill.

sides.position: The laugh of incredulity and unbelief - distrusting the promise by reason of her age, reproved by the LORD (vv.13-15; distinct from Abraham's laugh of faith, 17:17)
A laugh partly of pleasure/surprise mixed with weakness, her initial doubt giving way to faith (Hebrews 11:11)

themes: Therefore Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also?
Sarah's laughter at the promise (the crux of its meaning)
the laugh of incredulity and unbelief, reproved (distinct from Abraham's laugh of faith, 17:17)
her initial doubt giving way to faith (Hebrews 11:11)

schools.summary: The LORD reproves Sarah's laugh: 'and the LORD said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I of a surety bear a child, which am old?' — the LORD (the chief guest, now revealed as the LORD himself) knowing Sarah's inward laugh (v.12), though she laughed silently within herself (the divine knowledge of the heart, the LORD reproving her secret unbelief, a token that the speaker was God, who knows the secrets of the heart); the LORD's question reproving Sarah's laugh — her incredulity (v.12, the gentle but real rebuke of her unbelief, the LORD calling her to account for distrusting the promise, the reproof that would lead to her fear and denial, v.15); the LORD repeating the substance of Sarah's inward reasoning ('Shall I of a surety bear a child, which am old?') — showing that he knew her very thoughts (the divine searching of the heart, the LORD laying bare Sarah's secret doubt, the omniscience of the speaker) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I of a surety bear a child, which am old?
the LORD's reproof of Sarah's laughter
the LORD knowing her silent, inward laugh, a token of his deity
the divine searching of the heart, laying bare her secret doubt

schools.summary: Nothing too hard for the LORD: 'is any thing too hard for the LORD? At the time appointed I will return unto thee, according to the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son' — the LORD meeting Sarah's unbelief with the affirmation of his omnipotence ('Is any thing too hard, too wonderful, too difficult, for the LORD?', nothing too hard for God, Jeremiah 32:17, 27; Luke 1:37, 'with God nothing shall be impossible,' the answer to the natural impossibility, v.11 — the almighty God can do what nature cannot, the great truth that strengthens faith against every impossibility); the question rebuking unbelief and grounding faith (the believer to weigh the promise not by nature or human possibility but by the almighty power of God, the God who promised able to perform, Romans 4:21, the omnipotence of God the foundation of faith in his promises); the LORD repeating the definite promise (v.10) — at the appointed time, the time of life, next year, Sarah would have a son (the certain, dated promise reaffirmed, the assurance, grounded in God's omnipotence, that the impossible would be done) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Is any thing too hard for the LORD? At the time appointed I will return unto thee, according to the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son
the LORD's affirmation that nothing is too hard for him
the almighty God, for whom nothing is impossible (Luke 1:37; Jeremiah 32:17)
the omnipotence that grounds faith and assures the promise (Romans 4:21)

schools.summary: Sarah denies, the LORD rejoins: 'then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh' — Sarah, afraid (having heard the LORD reprove her secret laugh, v.13, and perceiving him to be more than a man), denying that she had laughed (the denial springing from fear, the fault compounded — the unbelief of the laugh now followed by the untruth of the denial, the weakness of Sarah in fear); Sarah's denial coming 'for she was afraid' (the fear of having her secret laugh and unbelief exposed, the dread of the divine presence, the fear that led her to deny, yet the denial a fault, an untruth, not to be excused); 'Nay; but thou didst laugh' (the LORD plainly contradicting her denial, affirming that she had laughed, the divine knowledge of the truth, the gentle but firm reproof, yet the LORD not withdrawing the promise, v.14 — Sarah's fault not annulling the grace, the mercy of God that bore with her weakness and still gave the son) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh
Sarah's denial of her laughter and the LORD's rejoinder
the fearful untruth met by the LORD's plain knowledge of the truth
the promise unwithdrawn, the grace that bore with her weakness

schools.summary: The men turn toward Sodom: 'and the men rose up from thence, and looked toward Sodom: and Abraham went with them to bring them on the way' — the visitors (the LORD and the two angels), having finished the meal and the promise, rising and turning their faces toward Sodom (the turn from the gracious visit to the errand of judgment, the doom of Sodom, vv.20-21; ch. 19, the men setting out toward the cities of the plain); 'Abraham went with them to bring them on the way' (the patriarch accompanying his guests part of the journey, the eastern courtesy of escorting departing guests, the continued hospitality and respect of Abraham, the walk that would bring the LORD's disclosure of Sodom's doom, v.17, and Abraham's intercession, vv.23-32); Abraham's escorting of the guests providing the occasion for the LORD to disclose his purpose against Sodom (v.17) and for Abraham's great intercession (the courtesy of the patriarch becoming the setting for the remarkable dialogue, the walk toward Sodom the prelude to the judgment and the prayer) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the men rose up from thence, and looked toward Sodom: and Abraham went with them to bring them on the way
the men turning toward Sodom and Abraham accompanying them
the turn from the gracious visit to the errand of judgment
the patriarch's escort the setting for the disclosure and intercession

schools.summary: The LORD will not hide from Abraham: 'and the LORD said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do' — the LORD deliberating (as it were, within himself) whether to conceal his purpose (the judgment of Sodom) from Abraham (the question expressing God's resolve to reveal his counsel to his friend, the divine condescension to make known his purpose to Abraham); God not hiding his purpose from Abraham — his friend (James 2:23, 'the friend of God'; 2 Chronicles 20:7; cf. Psalm 25:14, 'the secret of the LORD is with them that fear him'; John 15:15, 'I have called you friends... all things... I have made known unto you,' the intimacy of God with Abraham, the LORD sharing his counsel with his servant, as a man with his friend); 'that thing which I do' (the judgment God was about to bring on Sodom, vv.20-21, the LORD resolving to disclose it to Abraham, that he might know, and, as it proved, intercede, vv.23-32, the gracious revelation of God's purpose to his friend, and the occasion of the intercession) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said, Shall I hide from Abraham that thing which I do
the LORD's resolve not to hide his purpose from Abraham
the intimacy of God with his friend (James 2:23; John 15:15)
the disclosure of the judgment of Sodom, the occasion of the intercession

schools.summary: The reason from Abraham's destiny: 'seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?' — God's reason for revealing his purpose to Abraham: the patriarch to become a great and mighty nation (12:2; 17:4-6, the dignity and destiny of Abraham, the father of nations, the friend to whom God's counsel was fitly disclosed); 'all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him' the great gospel promise (12:3; 22:18) repeated (all nations blessed in Abraham, in his seed, Christ, Galatians 3:8, 16, the universal blessing through Abraham, the dignity of the patriarch, the channel of blessing to all nations, fitly made privy to God's counsel); because Abraham was to be so great (a mighty nation) and so blessed (the blessing of all nations in him), it being fitting that God should reveal his purpose to him (the friend and the father of the faithful made privy to the divine counsel, the dignity of Abraham the ground of the disclosure) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: Seeing that Abraham shall surely become a great and mighty nation, and all the nations of the earth shall be blessed in him?
the reason from Abraham's destiny - a great nation and the blessing of all nations
the gospel promise of universal blessing in Abraham's seed (12:3; Galatians 3:8)
the dignity of Abraham the fitting ground of the disclosure

schools.summary: God knows Abraham, who trains his house: 'for I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment; that the LORD may bring upon Abraham that which he hath spoken of him' — God knowing Abraham (with the knowledge of approval, choice, and intimate regard, 'know' in the sense of fore-ordaining love, cf. Amos 3:2; Romans 8:29, the LORD's gracious, electing knowledge of his servant, the ground, with v.18, of revealing his purpose to Abraham, God's special regard to the man he had chosen); God knowing Abraham would 'command his children and his household after him' to keep the way of the LORD, doing justice and judgment (the patriarch's faithful instruction of his household in true religion and righteousness, the duty of the godly head to train his children in the way of the LORD, Abraham commended as one who would govern his house in godliness); 'that the LORD may bring upon Abraham that which he hath spoken of him' (the purpose that God's promises to Abraham might be fulfilled, the godly training of his house a means by which the covenant-blessing would be carried on, the connection between Abraham's faithful instruction and the fulfillment of the promise, God's design that the blessing be continued through a godly seed) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: For I know him, that he will command his children and his household after him, and they shall keep the way of the LORD, to do justice and judgment
God's knowing of Abraham and his charge to train his household
God's gracious, electing knowledge of his servant (Romans 8:29)
the godly training of his house the means by which the covenant-blessing would be carried on

schools.summary: The cry and sin of Sodom: 'and the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous' — the cry (the outcry, the clamor) of the sins of Sodom and Gomorrah, or of the oppressed crying out against them, great (the wickedness of the cities crying to heaven, 19:13; cf. 4:10, the cry of Abel's blood, the great clamor of their sin reaching God); 'their sin is very grievous' (the sin of Sodom exceedingly grievous, heavy, abominable, the gross wickedness later named, ch. 19; Ezekiel 16:49-50; Jude 7, the ripened, enormous wickedness of the cities, the sin so great as to call down the judgment); the great cry and grievous sin of Sodom the ground of the coming judgment (v.21; ch. 19, the wickedness, full and crying to heaven, cf. 15:16, the iniquity full, provoking God's justice, the LORD's declaration of the cause before the judgment, the righteous ground of the destruction) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the LORD said, Because the cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is great, and because their sin is very grievous
the great cry and grievous sin of Sodom and Gomorrah
the wickedness crying to heaven (19:13; Ezekiel 16:49-50; Jude 7)
the righteous ground of the coming judgment

schools.summary: God's judicial investigation: 'I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know' — God speaking, after the manner of men (an anthropomorphism), of 'going down' to investigate (not that God, who is everywhere and knows all, must go or inquire, as at Babel, 11:5, but the language marking God's judicial care — that he judges on full knowledge, examining the case before he condemns, the pattern of just judgment); God would 'see whether they have done altogether according to the cry' (God, the righteous Judge, examining the truth of the case, not condemning on report, but on certain knowledge, the figure teaching that God judges justly, on full investigation, a model for human judges to inquire before they condemn); 'and if not, I will know' (God ascertaining the truth, though he already knew it, the language of judicial inquiry, the assurance that God's judgment rests on certain knowledge of the facts, the justice of God, who condemns none but on full and certain knowledge) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto me; and if not, I will know
the LORD's judicial investigation before judgment (an anthropomorphism)
God judging on full and certain knowledge, examining the case before condemning
the pattern of just judgment, a model for human judges

schools.summary: The angels go, Abraham stays before the LORD: 'and the men turned their faces from thence, and went toward Sodom: but Abraham stood yet before the LORD' — the two angels (of the three, vv.2; 19:1) departing toward Sodom (to execute the judgment and rescue Lot, ch. 19, the men setting out on the errand of judgment, the separation of the two from the LORD, who remained with Abraham); 'Abraham stood yet before the LORD' (the LORD, the chief of the three, who remained behind, staying with Abraham, the patriarch standing before the LORD, as a suppliant, in the posture of prayer and intercession, the setting for the great intercession, vv.23-32, Abraham, the friend of God, remaining in the divine presence; an ancient Jewish note held the original text read 'the LORD stood yet before Abraham,' softened by the scribes, but the received text, 'Abraham stood before the LORD,' is followed); Abraham's standing before the LORD the posture of intercessory prayer (the patriarch drawing near to plead for Sodom, and for Lot, the friend of God interceding, the occasion for the remarkable dialogue, vv.23-32) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill.

themes: And the men turned their faces from thence, and went toward Sodom: but Abraham stood yet before the LORD
the two angels going to Sodom while Abraham remained before the LORD
the LORD staying with Abraham, who stood as a suppliant
the setting for the great intercession (vv.23-32)

schools.summary: Abraham draws near to intercede: 'and Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?' — the patriarch drawing near to the LORD (in reverence and boldness) to make his intercession (the approach of the friend of God in prayer, the drawing near that marks the holy boldness and the humble reverence of Abraham's intercession, Hebrews 4:16, coming boldly to the throne of grace); Abraham's first plea, 'Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?' (the patriarch pleading for the righteous, the few godly, including Lot, who might be in Sodom, appealing to God's justice, that he would not destroy the righteous along with the wicked, the ground of the intercession — the distinction God makes between the righteous and the wicked, the compassion of Abraham for the city, and his concern for the righteous); Abraham's intercession a model of believing prayer — bold yet humble, importunate yet reverent (the patriarch pleading God's own justice and mercy, the friend of God interceding for the doomed city, the prayer that would press from fifty to ten, vv.24-32, the compassion and the faith of the intercessor) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham drew near, and said, Wilt thou also destroy the righteous with the wicked?
Abraham drawing near to intercede for the righteous
the patriarch's reverent boldness, pleading God's justice (Hebrews 4:16)
the model of believing intercession - bold yet humble, importunate yet reverent

schools.summary: Abraham pleads for fifty: 'peradventure there be fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou also destroy and not spare the place for the fifty righteous that are therein?' — Abraham beginning his intercession with fifty: would God destroy the city, or rather spare it, if fifty righteous were found in it? (the plea that, for the sake of the righteous, the whole place might be spared, the appeal to God's mercy and justice — that the righteous might be the means of sparing the wicked among whom they dwelt); Abraham beginning with fifty — a considerable number (the patriarch pleading that, if so many righteous were found, the city should be spared for their sake, the intercession opening with the larger number, to be pressed downward, vv.27-32); Abraham pleading that God would spare the place for the sake of the righteous in it (the principle that the righteous are a blessing and preservation to the place where they dwell, the salt of the earth, Matthew 5:13, the intercessor's appeal that the godly few might save the many, the ground of the plea) — Gill.

themes: Peradventure there be fifty righteous within the city: wilt thou also destroy and not spare the place for the fifty righteous that are therein?
Abraham's plea to spare the city for fifty righteous
the godly few a preservation to the place where they dwell (Matthew 5:13)
the intercession opening with the larger number, to be pressed downward

schools.summary: The Judge of all the earth will do right: 'that be far from thee to do after this manner, to slay the righteous with the wicked: and that the righteous should be as the wicked, that be far from thee: Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' — Abraham appealing that it was unthinkable for God to slay the righteous with the wicked, or to treat the righteous as the wicked (the appeal to God's justice, which distinguishes between the righteous and the wicked, the patriarch pleading that God's justice forbids such an indiscriminate destruction); 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' the foundation of the plea (the certain justice of God: God alone the supreme Judge of all the earth, the standard and source of justice and rectitude, he cannot do unrighteousness, Romans 3:5-6, 'Is God unrighteous? God forbid'; Deuteronomy 32:4; Psalm 11:7, the affirmation that God's justice is fixed and sure — he will assuredly do right, the truth on which Abraham rests his prayer, and on which the believer rests in all God's dealings, God will not slay the righteous with the wicked, for he is just); the question the great assurance of the believer — that God, the Judge of all, always does right (the rectitude of God in all his judgments, the ground of trust amid the mysteries of providence, God's justice the rock of faith) — Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: What does 'Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?' affirm?

sides.position: God alone is the supreme Judge, the standard of justice, who cannot do unrighteousness (Romans 3:5-6; Deuteronomy 32:4); his justice is fixed and sure - he will assuredly do right, distinguishing righteous from wicked - the foundation of the plea and the believer's trust

themes: That be far from thee... to slay the righteous with the wicked... Shall not the Judge of all the earth do right?
Abraham's appeal to the justice of the Judge of all the earth (the crux of divine justice)
God alone the supreme Judge, who cannot do unrighteousness (Romans 3:5-6; Deuteronomy 32:4)
God's justice fixed and sure, the foundation of the plea and the believer's trust

schools.summary: The LORD promises to spare for fifty: 'and the LORD said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sakes' — God granting Abraham's first plea (v.24): if fifty righteous were found in Sodom, he would spare the whole place for their sakes (the gracious answer to the intercession, the affirmation that God would spare the many for the sake of the righteous few); the condition — the finding of fifty righteous (God's readiness to spare the city if such were found, the mercy of God, willing to spare for the sake of the righteous); 'I will spare all the place for their sakes' (God would spare the whole city for the sake of the fifty righteous, the principle that the righteous are a preservation to the place, the readiness of God's mercy to spare for the godly few, the encouragement to Abraham to press his plea, vv.27-32, the grace of God in answering the intercessor) — Gill.

themes: And the LORD said, If I find in Sodom fifty righteous within the city, then I will spare all the place for their sakes
the LORD's promise to spare the city for fifty righteous
God willing to spare the many for the sake of the godly few
the principle that the righteous are a preservation to the place

schools.summary: Abraham's humble acknowledgment: 'and Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord, which am but dust and ashes' — Abraham acknowledging the boldness of his interceding with God (he had ventured, taken upon himself, to speak to the LORD, the holy boldness of the intercessor, yet joined with deep humility, the friend of God pressing his plea, conscious of his presumption); Abraham confessing himself 'but dust and ashes' (a frail, mortal creature, made of dust, 2:7; 3:19, unworthy to speak with God, the deep humility of the patriarch, the lowliness that accompanies true boldness in prayer, the intercessor abasing himself before the Most High); Abraham's words joining holy boldness with profound humility (he pressing his plea, yet owning his nothingness, the pattern of believing prayer — bold to ask, humble in asking, the reverence and the importunity of the intercessor held together, the model of how the creature pleads with the Creator) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Abraham answered and said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord, which am but dust and ashes
Abraham's humble acknowledgment as he presses his plea
holy boldness joined with deep humility, owning his frailty (2:7)
the pattern of believing prayer - bold to ask, humble in asking

schools.summary: Abraham pleads for forty-five: 'peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous: wilt thou destroy all the city for lack of five? And he said, If I find there forty and five, I will not destroy it' — Abraham, encouraged by God's answer (v.26), pressing his plea: if the fifty lacked five (forty-five), would God destroy the city for lack of five? and God assenting — he would not destroy it for forty-five (the intercession pressed lower, and the gracious answer granted); Abraham pleading the smallness of the difference — would God destroy the whole city for the lack of five righteous? (the patriarch pressing God's mercy by degrees, the intercession lowering the number); 'If I find there forty and five, I will not destroy it' (God assenting to spare the city for forty-five, the gracious yielding to the intercessor's plea, the readiness of God's mercy, the encouragement to Abraham to press still lower, vv.29-32, the patience of God in the dialogue) — Gill.

themes: Peradventure there shall lack five of the fifty righteous... And he said, If I find there forty and five, I will not destroy it
Abraham's plea for forty-five and God's assent
the patriarch pressing God's mercy by degrees
the readiness of God's mercy, yielding to the plea

schools.summary: Abraham pleads for forty: 'and he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there. And he said, I will not do it for forty's sake' — Abraham pressing the plea further, lowering the number to forty, and God assenting — he would not destroy the city for forty's sake (the persevering intercession, and the gracious answer, the patriarch pressing God's mercy by degrees); Abraham 'spake unto him yet again' (the persevering, importunate intercession, the patriarch not ceasing, but pressing his plea lower, the importunity of believing prayer, Luke 18:1-8, the friend of God continuing to plead); 'I will not do it for forty's sake' (God assenting to spare the city for forty, the continued yielding of God's mercy to the intercessor, the readiness of God to spare for the righteous, the patience and grace of God in the dialogue, encouraging Abraham to press still lower, vv.30-32) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And he spake unto him yet again, and said, Peradventure there shall be forty found there. And he said, I will not do it for forty's sake
Abraham's plea for forty and God's assent
the persevering, importunate intercession (Luke 18:1-8)
God's mercy graciously yielding to the plea

schools.summary: Abraham pleads for thirty: 'and he said unto him, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak: Peradventure there shall thirty be found there. And he said, I will not do it, if I find thirty there' — Abraham, pressing the plea to thirty, prefacing it with a humble entreaty that the Lord not be angry at his persistence, and God assenting — he would not destroy the city if thirty were found (the deepening humility of the intercessor, and the continued grace of God); Abraham, conscious of pressing God hard, entreating, 'let not the Lord be angry' (the deepening reverence and humility as he lowered the number, the intercessor's fear of presuming too far, the holy boldness tempered with growing awe, the patriarch's care not to offend in his importunity); 'I will not do it, if I find thirty there' (God assenting to spare the city for thirty, the continued yielding of God's mercy, the patience of God with the importunate intercessor, the readiness to spare for the righteous, the encouragement to press still lower, vv.31-32) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he said unto him, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak: Peradventure there shall thirty be found there. And he said, I will not do it, if I find thirty there
Abraham's plea for thirty, with deepening reverence
the holy boldness tempered with growing awe, fearing to presume too far
God's patience with the importunate intercessor

schools.summary: Abraham pleads for twenty: 'and he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord: Peradventure there shall be twenty found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for twenty's sake' — Abraham, again owning his boldness in speaking to the Lord (as v.27), pressing the plea to twenty, and God assenting — he would not destroy the city for twenty's sake (the persevering intercession, lowered again, and the continued grace of God); Abraham again acknowledging his presumption in pressing his plea (as v.27, the humble boldness of the intercessor, conscious of venturing far, the patriarch persevering, yet abasing himself); 'I will not destroy it for twenty's sake' (God assenting to spare the city for twenty, the continued, remarkable yielding of God's mercy to the intercessor, the patience and grace of God, lowering the number with Abraham, the readiness of God to spare the many for the few, the encouragement to the final plea, v.32) — Gill.

themes: And he said, Behold now, I have taken upon me to speak unto the Lord: Peradventure there shall be twenty found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for twenty's sake
Abraham's plea for twenty and God's assent
the humble boldness of the persevering intercessor
God's continued, remarkable yielding of mercy

schools.summary: Abraham's final plea for ten: 'and he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once: Peradventure ten shall be found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for ten's sake' — Abraham, with deep reverence ('let not the Lord be angry') and announcing this his last plea ('yet but this once'), pressing the number to ten, and God assenting — he would not destroy the city for ten's sake (the final step of the intercession, and the gracious answer); why Abraham ceased at ten variously explained (perhaps because he was confident that at least ten righteous, Lot's family, and others, would be found, so that the city would surely be spared, or in reverence, not daring to press God further, or because ten was held a sufficient number, a small remnant, to represent a godly presence, or because God's Spirit guided him to cease there, knowing the city's doom was fixed, there not being even ten, the intercession ceasing at ten, and the LORD ending the dialogue, v.33); Abraham's prayer not changing God's eternal purpose (God knew there were not ten righteous, and the city would fall, ch. 19), but revealing God's justice and mercy, displaying Abraham's compassion and faith, and being the appointed means by which Lot was delivered (19:29, 'God remembered Abraham, and sent Lot out,' the intercession the model of believing, importunate prayer, the patriarch's humanity and faith worthy of all admiration, the prayer that prevailed for Lot, though Sodom fell) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: Why did Abraham stop at ten, and what did the intercession accomplish?

sides.position: Abraham ceased from confidence that ten would be found (Lot's household), or in reverence not pressing further, or as ten was a sufficient remnant, or as guided by God (the doom being fixed)
The intercession did not change God's eternal purpose but revealed his justice and mercy, displayed Abraham's faith and compassion, and was the means by which Lot was delivered (19:29)

themes: And he said, Oh let not the Lord be angry, and I will speak yet but this once: Peradventure ten shall be found there. And he said, I will not destroy it for ten's sake
Abraham's final plea for ten and the close of the intercession (the crux of why it stopped at ten)
Abraham ceasing from confidence, reverence, or divine guidance
the intercession revealing God's justice, displaying Abraham's faith, and delivering Lot (19:29)

schools.summary: The LORD departs, Abraham returns: 'and the LORD went his way, as soon as he had left communing with Abraham: and Abraham returned unto his place' — when the intercession was ended (the plea having reached ten, v.32), the LORD departing (the close of the gracious communing, the theophany, the promise, and the intercession, the LORD going his way, to the judgment of Sodom, ch. 19, and the dialogue with Abraham ending); the LORD 'left communing with Abraham' (the remarkable communion of God with his friend, the familiar dialogue, the intercession, ended, the gracious condescension of God in communing with Abraham, the close of the wonderful interview); 'Abraham returned unto his place' (the patriarch returning home, to Mamre, 13:18, the intercession ended, Abraham going back, leaving the issue with God, the friend of God, having pleaded, returning in peace, committing Sodom, and Lot, to the just and merciful Judge, the close of the chapter, before the judgment of Sodom, ch. 19) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD went his way, as soon as he had left communing with Abraham: and Abraham returned unto his place
the close of the LORD's communing with Abraham
the LORD departing to the judgment of Sodom, the intercession ended
Abraham returning home, committing the issue to the just and merciful Judge
